coredata.dc:title: Arginine-derived carbon dots with antioxidant activity for treating aflatoxin B1-induced liver injury via Nrf2/Keap1 and NLRP3 pathways in mice
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Xuejing
Cheng, Jiuxiang
Yang, Yongshou
Wang, Jingmin
Wang, Yongzhong
coredata.dc:description: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) is a prevalent contaminant in food and feed matrices, known for its hepatotoxic effects. Its metabolic breakdown generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), leading to oxidative stress and subsequent liver damage. Mitigating oxidative stress is, therefore, essential for ameliorating the hepatocellular damage and systemic toxicity caused by AFB1. Here, we synthesized arginine carbon dots (Arg-CDs) with robust antioxidant properties through a simple hydrothermal method using arginine and citric acid. Our investigation demonstrated that Arg-CDs effectively mitigate oxidative stress in nematodes. Furthermore, in murine models of AFB1-induced hepatic injury, Arg-CDs effectively restored liver function, as evidenced by the improvement in histopathological features and biochemical markers. Notably, Arg-CDs administration upregulated the transcriptional activity of nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2), along with its downstream antioxidant effectors and phase II detoxifying enzymes under AFB1 exposure. Moreover, Arg-CDs alleviated hepatic inflammatory injury by modulating the NLRP3/Caspase-1/GSDMD-mediated pyroptosis pathway. Arg-CDs also demonstrated therapeutic potential in enhancing intestinal barrier function in AFB1-exposed mice. Collectively, these findings highlight the potential of Arg-CDs as a novel and biocompatible therapeutic modality for alleviating AFB1-induced hepatic and intestinal damage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Arginine carbon dots
Aflatoxin B1
Liver damage
Oxidative stress
Nrf2
NLRP3
Inflammation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo biocompatibility and antioxidant activity of Arg-CDs
RNA isolation and real-time quantitative PCR analysis (RT-qPCR)
Preparation and characterization of the Arg-CDs
Antioxidant and biocompatibility of the Arg-CDs
Arg-CDs alleviate juglone-induced oxidative stress damage in C. elegans
Arg-CDs ameliorate liver damage in mice exposed to AFB1
Arg-CDs alleviate AFB1-induced hepatic oxidative damage by activating the Nrf2 antioxidant pathway
Arg-CDs alleviate liver inflammatory by inhibiting NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated pyroptosis
Arg-CDs enhance the intestinal barrier function in AFB1-exposed mice

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Launching scientific research funds of Anhui University ( S020318003/009 ); the Natural Science Foundation of the Anhui Higher Education Institutions of China (JM Wang, No. KJ2021A0051 ); the postdoctoral fellow in Anhui province ( 9341129 ); and the Construction Funds of Anhui Province Joint Training Base for Postgraduates ( Z010110038 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Arg-CDs. (A) Graphical route of the synthesis of the Arg-CDs. (B) TEM image of the Arg-CDs (Scale bar = 10 nm). Inset: HR-TEM image (Scale bar = 5 nm), Particle size distribution of Arg-CDs. (C) XRD spectrum of the Arg-CDs. (D) FT-IR spectrum of the Arg-CDs. (E) XPS survey spectrum of the Arg-CDs. High resolution XPS of (F) C 1s, (G) N 1s and (H) O 1s.

Arg-CDs supplementation reduced oxidative stress damage in Juglone-induced C. elegans. (A) DPPH and (B) ABTS+ scavenging activity at different concentrations of Arg-CDs. Inset: Representative photos of different reaction concentrations. (C) Representative images (Scale bar = 500 μm) of nematodes body length. Statistics analysis of (D) body length and (E) movement frequency of nematodes within 30s. (F) DCFH-DA detection photos of ROS (Scale bar = 100 μm) and (G) the corresponding quantitative average fluorescence intensity. The levels of (H) SOD and (I) MDA in C. elegans. (J) The hemolysis ratio of Arg-CDs at various concentrations. Data are presented as mean ± SEM. Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's multiple comparisons test p < 0.05; groups sharing the same letter are not significantly different.

Arg-CDs supplementation ameliorate the liver injury in AFB1-induced mice. (A) An outline of the experimental scheme. (B) Final body weight and (C) liver weight of the experimental mice. (D) AST, ALT, and ALP levels in the serum. (E) H&E staining of liver tissue (Scale bar = 50 and 20 μm, respectively). The levels of (F) SOD and (G) CAT in the serum. The levels of (H) GSH and (I) MDA in the liver. (J) The relative mRNA expression levels of IL-6, IL-1β and TNF-α in the liver. Data are presented as mean ± SEM. Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's multiple comparisons test p < 0.05; groups sharing the same letter are not significantly different.

Arg-CDs protects against AFB1-induced hepatic oxidative damage by activating the Nrf2/Keap1 signaling pathway. (A) The relative protein expression level of CYP450 1A2 in the liver and (B) statistical analysis, with β-actin serving as an internal control (n = 3). The relative mRNA expression levels of (C) GST A3 and (D) Nrf2 in the liver (n = 5). (E) The relative mRNA expression levels of detoxification genes GSS, GCLC and GCLM in the liver (n = 5). (F) The relative mRNA expression levels of antioxidant genes SOD, CAT, HO-1 and NQO-1 in the liver (n = 5). (G) The relative protein expression levels of Nrf2, Keap1, GCLC and NQO-1 in the liver and (H) statistical analysis, with β-actin serving as an internal control (n = 3). (I) The relative protein expression levels of Nrf2 in the nucleus and cytoplasm in the liver and (J) statistical analysis, with Lamin B or β-actin serving as internal controls (n = 3). Values are expressed as mean ± SEM. Letters indicate statistical significance based on a one-way ANOVA with Tukey's multiple comparisons test (p < 0.05); samples sharing letters are not significantly different.

Arg-CDs inhibit the NLRP3/Caspase-1/GSDMD pathway to alleviate AFB1-induced inflammation. The relative mRNA expression levels of (A) TXNIP, (B) ASC, (C) Caspase-1, (D) GSDMD, (E) IL-1β and (F) IL-18 in the liver. (G) Western blotting of NLRP3 and Caspase-1 in the liver and (H) their bands' intensities were quantified and normalized as per those for β-actin. Data are presented as mean ± SEM. Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's multiple comparisons test p < 0.05; groups sharing the same letter are not significantly different.

Arg-CDs supplementation improved the intestinal barrier impairment in the ileum of AFB1-exposed mice. (A) Representative photos of small intestine and images of H&E staining of ileum sections (Scale bar = 100 μm). Statistical analysis of (B) small intestine length and (C) villus height/crypt depth ratio. (D) The relative mRNA expression levels of ZO-1, Occludin, Claudin-1 and Muc2 in the ileum. (G) Western blotting of IL-1β and Occludin in the ileum and (F, G) their bands' intensities were quantified and normalized as per those for β-actin. The LPS levels in the (H) serum and (I) liver. (J) The relative mRNA expression levels of IL-6, IL-1β and TNF-α in the ileum. Data are presented as mean ± SEM. Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's multiple comparisons test p < 0.05; groups sharing the same letter are not significantly different.
The Schematic representation of the preparation of Arg-CDs and its therapeutic mechanism AFB1-induced liver injury.

head.ce:title: Arginine-derived carbon dots with antioxidant activity for treating aflatoxin B1-induced liver injury via Nrf2/Keap1 and NLRP3 pathways in mice

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Human Microenvironment and Precision Medicine of Anhui Higher Education Institutes, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, Anhui, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Human Microenvironment and Precision Medicine of Anhui Higher Education Institutes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Human Microenvironment and Precision Medicine of Anhui Higher Education Institutes, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, Anhui, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Province Joint Construction Discipline Key Laboratory of Nanobody Technology, Hefei, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Province Joint Construction Discipline Key Laboratory of Nanobody Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Province Joint Construction Discipline Key Laboratory of Nanobody Technology, Hefei, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Healcurer Heath Biotech Co., Ltd. – Anhui University Joint Postgraduate Training Base of Anhui Province, Hefei, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Healcurer Heath Biotech Co., Ltd. – Anhui University Joint Postgraduate Training Base of Anhui Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Healcurer Heath Biotech Co., Ltd. – Anhui University Joint Postgraduate Training Base of Anhui Province, Hefei, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Life Sciences, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) is a prevalent contaminant in food and feed matrices, known for its hepatotoxic effects. Its metabolic breakdown generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), leading to oxidative stress and subsequent liver damage. Mitigating oxidative stress is, therefore, essential for ameliorating the hepatocellular damage and systemic toxicity caused by AFB1. Here, we synthesized arginine carbon dots (Arg-CDs) with robust antioxidant properties through a simple hydrothermal method using arginine and citric acid. Our investigation demonstrated that Arg-CDs effectively mitigate oxidative stress in nematodes. Furthermore, in murine models of AFB1-induced hepatic injury, Arg-CDs effectively restored liver function, as evidenced by the improvement in histopathological features and biochemical markers. Notably, Arg-CDs administration upregulated the transcriptional activity of nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2), along with its downstream antioxidant effectors and phase II detoxifying enzymes under AFB1 exposure. Moreover, Arg-CDs alleviated hepatic inflammatory injury by modulating the NLRP3/Caspase-1/GSDMD-mediated pyroptosis pathway. Arg-CDs also demonstrated therapeutic potential in enhancing intestinal barrier function in AFB1-exposed mice. Collectively, these findings highlight the potential of Arg-CDs as a novel and biocompatible therapeutic modality for alleviating AFB1-induced hepatic and intestinal damage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aflatoxins are primarily biosynthesized as secondary metabolites by fungi such as Aspergillus flavus and Aspergillus parasiticus, frequently contaminating various food and feed sources [1,2]. Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) is particularly concerning due to its classification as a Group 1 carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer [3]. Preventing AFB1 exposure remains challenging, leading to its accumulation in human and animal bodies through the food chain, resulting in multi-organ damage [4,5]. The liver, being a primary site for detoxification, is especially susceptible to AFB1 toxicity, with the cytochrome P450 enzyme system (CYP450) playing a crucial role in metabolizing AFB1 into the carcinogenic metabolite AFB1-exo-8,9-epoxide (AFBO) [6,7]. This process triggers the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), contributing to the development of liver disease. Therefore, the development of a safe and effective approach to prevent or treat AFB1-induced hepatotoxicity is urgently needed.

Oxidative stress plays a crucial role in the onset and progression of AFB1-induced liver damage [8]. AFB1 metabolism in the liver generates excessive ROS, overwhelming the body's antioxidant defense system and resulting in hepatic cell apoptosis and liver damage. Notably, nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2) is a pivotal transcription factor known to regulate various oxidative stress related diseases [9]. Recent studies have suggested that targeting Nrf2 holds promise in alleviating AFB1-induced oxidative stress [10]. Under normal circumstances, Nrf2 binds to kelch-like ECH-associated protein 1 (Keap1) in the cytoplasm, preventing its nuclear translocation and transcriptional activity. However, exposure to oxidants and electrophiles, such as AFBO and ROS, induces the dissociation of Nrf2 from Keap1, leading to its activation [11]. Activated Nrf2 then translocates to the nucleus, where it binds to antioxidant response elements (ARE) and promotes the expression of downstream phase II detoxifying enzymes and antioxidant proteins. Strategies involving the removal of ROS by antioxidants and activation of the antioxidant system hold potential for preventing and treating AFB1 toxicity.

Nanomaterials have shown promise in antioxidative therapy for a wide range of diseases through scavenging excessive ROS [12,13]. For example, Ling et al. reported that cerium nanoenzymes can effectively clear ROS and generate rich O2, offering dual benefits in detoxification and inflammation regulation in liver injury treatment [14]. While metal- or metal oxide-based nanomaterials have been beneficial in biological therapy, concerns about potential long-term toxicity from released metal ions have prompted a shift towards carbon-based nanomaterials known for their excellent biocompatibility [15–17].

Carbon nanodots (CDs), a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterials, are gaining popularity in the biomedical field due to their straightforward production process, cost-effectiveness, low toxicity and strong antioxidant properties [17,18]. Researchers have successfully synthesized CDs with antioxidant properties from various sources such as glutathione and biotin to address conditions like colitis [19]. Additionally, CDs derived from natural products such as broccoli water extracts [20] and coptidis rhizome [21] have shown protective effects against diseases related to oxidative stress. In the realm of liver injury research, CDs have garnered significant attention for their potential in antioxidant therapy. Studies have shown promising results, such as integrating platinum nanoparticles with carbon nanodots to create a potent nanozyme for alleviating liver oxidative damage induced by CCl4 [22]. However, challenges such as potential toxicity issues from organic solvents and metal introduction, as well as issues with high-temperature pyrolysis, limit the broader application of CDs in liver injury treatment. Thus, developing metal-free doped CDs with high biocompatibility and antioxidant activity presents an effective strategy for mitigating AFB1-induced liver toxicity. This study aims to investigate the ameliorating effect of arginine-derived CDs (Arg-CDs) on AFB1-induced liver injury in mice and elucidate the underlying mechanism.

The synthesis of arginine derived Arg-CDs was carried out using a one-pot hydrothermal method. In brief, 4 g of arginine and 4 g of citric acid were added to 40 mL of double distilled water (ddH2O). The solution was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, followed by heating at 200 °C for 12 h. Following naturally cooling to room temperature, the resulting products were filtered using a filtration membrane (0.22 μm). Subsequently, to enhance the purify of Arg-CDs, the solution underwent dialysis (with a 500 kDa molecular weight cut-off) in deionized water for 48 h. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain a powdered form for further use.

The morphology of samples was analyzed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained from a D8-Focus X-ray diffractometer (Brook, German). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of Arg-CDs was conducted using a Vertex 80 + Hyperion 2000 spectrometer (Karlsruhe, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Electron ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (New York, USA) to analyze surface functional groups.

The antioxidant capacity of Arg-CDs was assessed with the ABTS and DPPH methods [23]. Specifically, the DPPH free radical (DPPH•) scavenging activity was determined as follows: Various concentrations of aqueous solutions of Arg-CDs were mixed with 1 mL of 0.05 mM DPPH• ethanol solution to achieve final concentrations of 0, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 μg/mL of Arg-CDs. After a 20-min incubation in darkness, the absorbance values (
A i
) at 517 nm were recorded for each group of solutions. The DPPH• scavenging activity was calculated using the formula:
1 −
A i
−
A j
/
A c
× 100 % #
where
A j
represents the absorbance value of the DPPH• system in place of anhydrous ethanol, and
A c
represents the absorbance value of the Arg-CDs solution system replaced by ddH2O.

The ABTS free radical (ABTS•) scavenging activity was determined by first preparing dark green ABTS solution through the reaction of ABTS stock solution (14 mM) with potassium persulfate (K+ 2S2O8) solution (4.9 mM) over 12 h in darkness. The resulting ABTS solution was then diluted with ddH2O until the absorbance at 734 nm reached approximately 1.0. Subsequently, different concentrations of Arg-CDs solution (ranging from 0 to 500 μg/mL) were added to 1 mL of the ABTS solution. The mixture was incubated in darkness at 37 °C for 6 min, and the absorbance at 734 nm was measured as indicator (
A i
). The ABTS• scavenging activity was calculated using the formula:
1 −
A i
−
A j
/
A c
× 100 % #
where
A j
represents the absorbance value of the ABTS system replaced by ddH2O, and
A c
represents the absorbance value of ABTS at 734 nm without the presence of Arg-CDs.

Wild-type N2 C. elegans were sourced from the College of Life Sciences, University of Science and Technology of China. Nematode growth medium (NGM) containing E. coli OP50 served as the culture substrate for the nematode at an incubator temperature of 20 °C [24].

Plates with a high density of nematode eggs were preferentially chosen for culture. The NGM plates were washed with sterile M9 buffer, and the collected rinsing solution was centrifuged to remove the supernatant. The resulting pellet was then treated with nematode lysate and sterile M9 buffer, following by centrifugation at 1000 rpm at 4 °C for 5 min, with the subsequent removal of the supernatant. The pellet was washed with M9 buffer, re-suspended, and transferred to an NGM plate containing E. coli OP50 for cultivation in a constant temperature incubator at 20 °C. After 24 h, the eggs hatched, and approximately 48 h later, the nematode reached L4 stage, indicating successful synchronization for subsequent experimental procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biocompatibility and antioxidant activity of Arg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the control group, the nematodes were fed E. coli OP50, whereas in the experimental group, they were fed a mixture of OP50 and Arg-CDs (400 μg/mL) at designated concentrations. Synchronized L4-stage nematodes were then evenly distributed onto separate NGM plate, which each group containing a minimum of 3 plates, and approximately 100 nematodes per plate. The plates were selected randomly for the assessment of body length and motion count.

The nematodes at the spawning stage were divided to retrieve eggs, which were then distributed into the control group (NGM plate), model group (NGM plate with 0.5 mM juglone) and intervention group (NGM plate with 0.5 mM juglone and 400 μg/mL Arg-CDs). Following 4 days of culture, a ROS detection probe was used to monitor the ROS levels in each group. ROS fluorescence images were captured using laser confocal microscopy (OLYMPUS FV 1000 + IX 81). Nematodes from each experimental group were collected, and protein was extracted to assess the levels of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and malondialdehyde (MDA).

The blood compatibility of Arg-CDs was assessed using a hemolysis assay. Mouse blood (1 mL) was collected and centrifuged to isolate the red blood cells (RBCs). A 40 μL suspension of RBCs was then added to 960 μL of Arg-CDs solutions at varying concentrations (100, 200, 400, and 800 μg/mL) and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. RBCs treated with PBS solution served as the negative control, while those treated with water served as the positive control. After incubation, the mixtures were centrifuged, and the absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 543 nm. The hemolysis rate was calculated using the following formula [25].
Hemolysis ratio
%
=
A sample
−
A negative
A positive
−
A negative
× 100 %
where
A sample
,
A positive
and
A negative
represent the absorbance of RBC suspensions treated with Arg-CDs, water and PBS solution, respectively.

Fifteen C57BL/6 male mice (6-week-old, 18–22 g) were purchased from Hefei Qingyuan Biotechnology Company (Anhui, China). The mice were maintained in a controlled environment with artificial lighting (12-h light/dark cycles), constant temperature (25 °C), and humidity levels (50 ± 5 %), and provided ad libitum access to food and water during a 7-day acclimation period. The animal experiments conducted in this study were approved by the Committee for the Care and Use of Experimental Animals at Anhui University.

The mice were randomly assigned to three groups (n = 5 per group): control group, received 5 % DMSO or sterile phosphate-buffered saline (PBS); the AFB1 group was orally administered AFB1 at 0.75 mg/kg body weight (BW), with AFB1 powder dissolved in 5 % DMSO; and the AFB1 plus Arg-CDs group (AFB1 + Arg-CDs) received orally administration of Arg-CDs at 8 mg/kg BW, 8 h before AFB1 administration. The mice BW and food intake were recorded weekly. After 4 weeks of treatments, mice were anesthetized with narcolan, and whole blood samples were collected. The serum was obtained by centrifugation (4 °C, 2500 rpm) for 20 min and storage at −80 °C for further analysis. Liver tissues were excised and weighed, and the small intestine and colon tissues were collected and measured for length. These tissues were stored at −80 °C for subsequent analysis.

The serum biochemical factors indicative of hepatic damage, such as alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) were quantified using commercial assay kits (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, China). Lipopolysaccharides (LPS) levels in serum and liver were determined by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) following the manufacturer's recommended protocol.

The activities of SOD, catalase (CAT) in the serum samples were assessed. Liver tissues were homogenized in pre-cooled saline solution, and the resulting 10 % homogenized solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C. The obtained supernatants were used for determining the activities of glutathione (GSH) and MDA concentrations following the instructions of the corresponding commercial kits (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, China).

The ileum and liver tissues were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde overnight and subsequently embedded in paraffin blocks. Sections of five micrometer thickness were prepared and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). The Image J software was used to measure the length of ileum villus and crypt depth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA isolation and real-time quantitative PCR analysis (RT-qPCR)
ce:section.ce:para: Total RNA was extracted from frozen liver and ileum tissues using Trizol Reagent (Monad, Jiangsu, China) following the manufacturer's instructions. The concentration and purity of RNA were determined with a Microplate Reader, with samples having an A260/A280 ration between 1.8 and 2.0 considered to be of acceptable quality and integrity. Subsequently, the RNA was reverse transcribed into cDNA using the reverse transcription system kit (Monad, Jiangsu, China). RT-qPCR analysis was performed using SYBR Green Master Mix (Monad, Jiangsu, China) on an Applied Biosystems Quant Studio system (Thermo Fisher, Germering, Germany). The relative expression of the target genes was calculated using the relative standard curve, with 18S rRNA serving as the reference gene. The primers sequences corresponding to the genes investigated in the present study are listed in Table S1.

Total RNA was extracted from frozen liver and ileum tissues using Trizol Reagent (Monad, Jiangsu, China) following the manufacturer's instructions. The concentration and purity of RNA were determined with a Microplate Reader, with samples having an A260/A280 ration between 1.8 and 2.0 considered to be of acceptable quality and integrity. Subsequently, the RNA was reverse transcribed into cDNA using the reverse transcription system kit (Monad, Jiangsu, China). RT-qPCR analysis was performed using SYBR Green Master Mix (Monad, Jiangsu, China) on an Applied Biosystems Quant Studio system (Thermo Fisher, Germering, Germany). The relative expression of the target genes was calculated using the relative standard curve, with 18S rRNA serving as the reference gene. The primers sequences corresponding to the genes investigated in the present study are listed in Table S1.

To extract total protein, liver or small intestine tissues were homogenized in a PBS solution and then lysed in RIPA buffer (YEASEN, Shanghai, China) containing 1 mM PMSF. Cytoplasmic and nuclear proteins were specifically extracted from the liver tissues using protein extraction kits (Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology, Shanghai, China), according to the manufacturer's protocol. The tissue protein concentration was determined using a BCA assay kit (Beyotime Biotechnology, Shanghai, China). The protein extract was combined with protein loading buffer and completely denatured in a boiling water bath. Subsequently, the protein samples were separated by SDS-PAGE, transferred onto a PVDF membrane, and the membrane was blocked PVDF in 5 % skim milk for 2 h. Following this, overnight incubation at 4 °C with a 1:1000 dilution of primary antibody was conducted. The membrane was then incubated with an HRP-conjugated secondary antibody at 37 °C for 1 h. Protein bands were visualized using an ECL chemiluminescent imaging kit (YEASEN, Shanghai, China) on a chemiluminescence imager (UVItec, Cambridge, UK). The expression levels of protein bands were quantified using Image J software, with β-actin or Lamin B serving as controls.

The experimental results were represented as means ± standard error (mean ± SEM) and analyzed using GraphPad Prism (version 8.3.4). Statistical significance was determined using one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's multiple comparisons test, with a significance threshold of p < 0.05. Groups sharing the same letter were considered not significantly different.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the Arg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of Arg-CDs was achieved using a hydrothermal method utilizing arginine and citric acid (Fig. 1A). Subsequently, the morphology of the obtained Arg-CDs was characterized by TEM analysis. The TEM images (Fig. 1B) revealed that the prepared Arg-CDs exhibited a well-monodispersed dispersion, with a roughly spherical shape and an average particle size of 4.02 ± 0.82 nm, demonstrating a Gaussian distribution within the range of 2–7 nm. Moreover, the HRTEM images showed clear lattice fringes of Arg-CDs, with an average d-spacing of 0.21 nm [26]. The XRD spectrum (Fig. 1C) exhibited an intense diffraction peak at 2θ = 20° [27], corresponding to the planes of graphite lattice spacing (002), indicating the graphite-like structure of Arg-CDs. The functional groups present on the surface of Arg-CDs were identified by FTIR spectroscopy. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1D) displayed a strong and broad near-infrared absorption band within the range of 2900–3500cm−1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH on the surface of the Arg-CDs. In addition, peaks at 1569cm−1 and 1658 cm−1 were ascribed to the presence of NH and CO functional groups, respectively [19]. To further validate the FTIR analysis results, XPS analysis was employed to determine the elemental types and valence states of the Arg-CDs. The three major peaks of C (60.27 At.%), O (28.11 At.%), and N (11.62 At.%) were found in the XPS spectra (Fig. 1E). In the high-resolution XPS spectrum, the C1s band was resolved into three peaks (Fig. 1F), corresponding to C=O/C=N (288.3 eV), CO (286.0 eV) and CC (284.8 eV), respectively. The N1s band exhibited two peaks at 401.8 and 399.9eV (Fig. 1G), indicating the presence of graphite-N and pyrrolic-N forms. Moreover, the O1s spectra displayed peaks at 532.6 and 532.0 eV, corresponding to C-O-H and CO functionalities, respectively (Fig. 1H) [28]. Both XPS and FTIR results confirmed the presence of abundant functional groups on the surface of the prepared Arg-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and biocompatibility of the Arg-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the antioxidant capacity of the Arg-CDs, the clearance ability of Arg-CDs on free radicals (ABTS+ and DPPH•) was determined. Fig. 2A and B demonstrates that increasing concentrations of Arg-CDs led to a rise in the inhibition rate of ABTS+ and DPPH•, with inhibition rates exceeding 80 % and 60 % at 400 μg/mL, respectively. These results show the exhibit excellent antioxidant properties of Arg-CDs.

The biocompatibility of Arg-CDs, a vital consideration for clinical applications, was evaluated using the model organism C. elegans. Body length and movement frequency were assessed in worms fed with E. coli OP50 and OP50 + 400 μg/mL Arg-CD for 7 days until reaching the synchronized L4 stage. The results presented in Fig. 2C and D indicate that there was no significant difference in the body length between the control group and the Arg-CD intervention group, with both reaching approximately 1000 μm. Similarly, the locomotion frequency of C. elegans showed no significant difference between the two groups (Fig. 2E). These findings indicate that Arg-CDs have minimal impact on the growth and motility of C. elegans, suggesting favorable biocompatibility. Moreover, as good blood biocompatibility is essential for in vivo applications, the hemocompatibility of Arg-CDs was assessed via a hemolysis test. The in vitro hemolysis experiment revealed that Arg-CDs at a concentration of 800 μg/mL exhibited a hemolysis rate of (Fig. 2J), further confirming their excellent biocompatibility. The safe and effective antioxidant properties of Arg-CDs highlight their potential as a potential therapeutic option for conditions associated with oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Arg-CDs alleviate juglone-induced oxidative stress damage in C. elegans
ce:section.ce:para: Biological organisms usually produce oxygen radicals, including H2O2, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide anion radicals (O2•−) as part of metabolic processes. However, excessive oxidative stress can lead to an imbalance in free radicals impacting gene transcription levels and resulting in elevated production of MDA, ROS, and other harmful compounds, ultimately exacerbating aging and immune dysfunction [29]. To establish an oxidative stress model, synchronized worms were exposed to 0.5 mM juglone, with an additional 200 μg/mL of Arg-CDs provided to the intervention group to evaluate its antioxidant capacity. The extent of oxidative damage was assessed by measuring fluorescence intensity in C. elegans using ROS fluorescent probes (DCFH-DA). As shown in Fig. 2F, the fluorescence intensity in the experimental group was lower than that in the model group under fluorescence microscopy. Image J analysis results in Fig. 2G further confirm the reduction in average fluorescence intensity in the experimental group, indicating the ability of Arg-CDs to mitigate excessive ROS levels in C. elegans.

Subsequent analysis using commercial assay kits determined SOD and MDA levels within C. elegans. SOD, an essential antioxidant enzyme, reflects the organism's antioxidant capacity, while MDA, the lipid peroxidation end product, serves as an indicator of oxidative damage. As depicted in Fig. 2H, the SOD levels in nematodes increased significantly following Arg-CDs intervention compared to the model group. Besides, the MDA level in C. elegans were notably reduced after Arg-CDs administration (Fig. 2I). These findings collectively demonstrate that Arg-CDs treatment improved the antioxidant capacity of C. elegans, effectively alleviating oxidative damage within the organism, suggesting that Arg-CDs is a potential nanomedicine for treating diseases related to oxidative damage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Arg-CDs ameliorate liver damage in mice exposed to AFB1
ce:section.ce:para: AFB1 exposure is a significant risk factor for hepatocellular carcinoma, a prevalent primary liver malignancy worldwide [30]. In this study, we investigated the therapeutic effects of Arg-CDs in an AFB1-induced mice liver injury model. The experimental schemes are shown in Fig. 3A. After 4-week experimental period, it was evident that the final body weight and liver weight of the AFB1 group were lower compared to those of the control group (Fig. 3B & C). However, the AFB1 + Arg-CDs group showed significantly higher final body weight and liver weight compared to the AFB1 group. The liver, being the primary target organ of AFB1 action, was assessed for biochemical parameters associated with liver damage following AFB1 exposure. Serum activities of ALT, AST, and ALP are sensitive markers of liver damage, with elevated levels indicate hepatocyte damage, and altered membrane permeability [31]. The AFB1 group showed elevated levels of these enzymes compared to the control group, indicative of potential liver damage (Fig. 3D). In contrast, the AFB1 + Arg-CDs group displayed significantly decreased levels of ALT, AST, and ALP compared to the AFB1 group (Fig. 3D).

Histopathological examination of the liver tissue following AFB1 treatment revealed significant liver damage (Fig. 3E), characterized by hepatocyte injury, nuclear condensation, increased pigment deposition, and cytoplasmic vacuolization. Notably, the AFB1 + Arg-CDs group exhibited reduced vacuolization, indicating alleviation of hepatocyte damage.

AFB1-induced ROS production contributes to oxidative damage initiation, leading to lipid peroxidation and MDA formation. AFB1 exposure resulted in increased liver MDA content, indicative of oxidative stress. Additionally, AFB1 inhibited the activities of antioxidant enzymes SOD, GSH, and CAT. However, treatment with Arg-CDs counteracted the inhibitory effect of AFB1 on antioxidant enzyme activity, concurrently reducing MDA levels (Fig. 3F-I).

Oxidative stress and inflammation are closely linked, with ROS and oxidative stress byproducts enhancing pro-inflammatory responses. AFB1-induced inflammation can exacerbate liver damage by upregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α (Fig. 3J). Following the Arg-CDs intervention, a significant downregulation of pro-inflammatory factor expression was observed, suggesting that Arg-CDs may mitigate AFB1-induced liver damage by modulating oxidative stress and inflammatory responses (Fig. 3J).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Arg-CDs alleviate AFB1-induced hepatic oxidative damage by activating the Nrf2 antioxidant pathway
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the mechanism by which Arg-CDs alleviate liver oxidative damage, the study delved into potential pathways involved. The phase I metabolic enzymes, such as CYP450s, are responsible for metabolizing AFB1 into harmful compounds. Western blot results showed that a significant increase in CYP1A2 protein expression in mouse liver after AFB1 exposure, with Arg-CDs showing a slight downregulation of this protein expression (Fig. 4A & B). In Phase II metabolism, detoxifying enzymes like glutathione S-transferase (GST), specifically GSTA3, play a crucial role in mitigating AFB1 toxicity [32,33]. Our study observed that Arg-CDs significantly reversed the decreased expression of GSTA3 induced by AFB1 (Fig. 4C).

The nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2)/ kelch-like ECH-associated protein 1 (Keap1) signaling pathway has a well-established protective role in hepatotoxicity caused by AFB1 [34,35]. As shown in the Fig. 4D, E, and H, Arg-CDs intervention promoted the dissociation of Keap1 and Nrf2, which is reflected in the significant increase of Nrf2 gene expression and the significant decrease of Keap1 protein expression. To further investigate the distribution of Nrf2 protein, its expression in the nucleus and cytoplasm in liver tissues was measured. The results indicated that nuclear Nrf2 protein expression was slightly elevated in the AFB1 + Arg-CDs group compared to the AFB1 group (p = 0.076), whereas cytoplasmic Nrf2 protein expression showed no significant difference between these two groups (Fig. 4I & J). Additionally, the expression of downstream detoxifying enzyme genes (GSS, GCLC, GCLM) and antioxidant enzyme genes (SOD, CAT, HO-1, NQO1) was significantly upregulated after Arg-CDs intervention (Fig. 4E & F). Western blot results further confirmed these findings, supporting that Arg-CDs alleviate liver oxidative damage by activating the Nrf2/Keap1-mediated oxidative stress pathway (Fig. 4G &H).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Arg-CDs alleviate liver inflammatory by inhibiting NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated pyroptosis
ce:section.ce:para: Thioredoxin interaction protein (TXNIP), a redox regulatory protein, has been linked to trigger NLRP3 inflammasome-mediated pyroptosis in response to various signals such as oxidative stress and AFB1. Our data revealed that Arg-CDs significantly downregulated the mRNA expression of TXNIP in AFB1-exposed mice (Fig. 5A), suggesting potential inhibition of the downstream pyroptotic pathway. Analysis of mRNA expression levels of genes related to the pyroptosis signaling pathway in the liver showed that AFB1 upregulated the expression of ASC, Caspase-1 and GSDMD in the liver, indicating the involvement of pyroptosis in liver cell damage in AFB1-exposed mice. However, the expression of these genes was significantly reversed after Arg-CDs intervention (Fig. 5B-D). Activated caspase-1 can cleave GSDMD, inducing the formation of cell membrane pores, leading to the release of interleukin-1β (IL-1β) and interleukin-18 (IL-18), contributing to cell death associated with inflammatory liver injury [36]. Therefore, the leakage of intracellular pro-inflammatory cytokines is an important feature of pyroptosis. Additionally, the mRNA expression of IL-18 and IL-1β in the AFB1 group was extremely higher than that in the control group, whereas the IL-1β and IL-18 levels in the AFB1 + Arg-CDs group were notably lower than those in the AFB1 group (Fig. 5E & F). In addition, Western blot results showed that the Arg-CDs intervention down-regulated the protein expression levels of NLRP3 and Caspase-1 compared to the AFB1 group (Fig. 5G & H). These results suggest that Arg-CDs alleviates AFB1-induced liver inflammation by inhibiting pyroptosis, highlighting a potential therapeutic mechanism for mitigating inflammatory liver damage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Arg-CDs enhance the intestinal barrier function in AFB1-exposed mice
ce:section.ce:para: Research has indicated a correlation between AFB1-induced liver injury and the disruption of intestinal barrier function. As depicted in Fig. 2A-C, treatment with Arg-CDs significantly increased the small intestinal length, the villus height, while reducing the crypt depth, resulting in a higher ratio of villus height to crypt depth in the ileum compared to the AFB1 group. Furthermore, we evaluated the expression of intestinal tight junction molecules at the mRNA and protein levels. The relative expression levels of intestinal tight junction molecules (ZO-1), Claudin-1, Occludin, and Muc2 were significantly decreased by AFB1 exposure, reflecting impaired intestinal barrier integrity (Fig. 6D-G). Remarkably, administration of Arg-CDs reversed these effects, highlighting the beneficial role of Arg-CDs in ameliorating intestinal barrier damage in AFB1-exposed mice. Impairment in the intestinal barrier can lead to the leakage of harmful substances from the intestine, such as LPS. Our research demonstrated that AFB1 exposure elevated LPS levels in both serum and liver tissue, which were reversed by Arg-CDs intervention (Fig. 6H & I). Additionally, Arg-CDs intervention significantly decreased the expression of inflammatory cytokines like IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α in the ileum (Fig. 6J), thereby alleviating intestinal inflammation in AFB1-exposed mice. These findings suggest that Arg-CDs may regulate intestinal barrier function to prevent the leakage of toxic substances from the intestine, thereby mitigating oxidative stress and inflammatory reactions in the body and reducing organ damage, including liver impairment.

The prevalence of AFB1 contamination in common raw materials utilized in food and feed, including corn, soybeans, peanuts, and wheat, remains a global concern [1,2]. The challenge of completely avoiding AFB1 intake persists due to the lack of economically feasible and truly effective methods for mold prevention and detoxification [5]. Consequently, AFB1 can enter the food chain and ultimately accumulate in the livers of both humans and animals via circulatory metabolism, leading to oxidative stress, inflammation, and apoptosis, all of which severely impair liver functions [4]. Therefore, it is critical to counteract AFB1 intoxication to protect liver health in both humans and animals.

Previous studies have indicated that antioxidants boost the effectiveness of antioxidant enzymes in both human and animal bodies [37,38], thereby ameliorating oxidative damage caused by AFB1. The introduction of natural enzymes can convert harmful ROS into harmless byproducts, effectively reducing oxidative harm induced by AFB1 [35]. Nevertheless, the limited stability, purification challenges, and high production expenses of natural enzymes significantly hinder their widespread application. Furthermore, non-enzymatic antioxidants sourced from medicinal plants such as Licorice Chalcone A and silymarin [39–41], possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that alleviate liver damage caused by AFB1. Nonetheless, the extraction of natural products is often intricate, characterized by low yields and lengthy processes. In recent years, the emergence of metal-free doped CDs with high biocompatibility and antioxidant activity, due to their simple production process, cost-effectiveness, stability and low toxicity, presents a promising alternative to natural enzymes and non-enzymatic antioxidants for treating diseases related to oxidative stress [42,43]. With ongoing exploration of CDs in antioxidant therapy, their alleviating effects on liver injury models have also received widespread attention, although there are limited reports on the CDs in the treatment of liver injury induced by AFB1. In view of this, we synthesized a novel Arg-CDs with a good antioxidant capacity. We used nematode model to verify their safety and assessed their antioxidant effect. Based on the remarkable effect of Arg-CDs in the oxidative stress model of nematodes, we further applied them to an AFB1-induced mouse model. Our investigations demonstrated that Arg-CDs significantly improving liver function by reducing oxidative stress, inflammation and pyrosis of hepatocytes, which partly depend on the activation of Nrf2 pathways.

Liver damage caused by AFB1 is closely related to severe liver toxicity, primarily induced by oxidative stress and inflammatory response of hepatocytes, which may impair liver function. Our study evaluated the therapeutic effect of Arg-CDs in an AFB1-induced liver injury model by measuring serum aminotransferase (ALT, AST, and ALP) and antioxidant oxidase (SOD, GSH, and CAT) levels. Elevated levels of these aminotransferase in serum indicate degenerative changes in the liver, hepatocyte damage, and altered hepatocyte membrane permeability [31]. These findings suggest that Arg-CDs successfully protects liver tissue from AFB1 toxicity. Furthermore, our results indicate Arg-CDs effectively inhibited the expression of the phase I metabolic enzyme CYP1A2 protein, which play a crucial role in converting AFB1 into its toxic metabolite AFBO. Additionally, Arg-CDs significantly reversed the decreased gene expression of GSTA3 in liver induced by AFB1, highlighting their potential in mitigating AFB1-induced hepatotoxicity through Phase II metabolism.

Previous studies have demonstrated that many antioxidants regulate the expression of various antioxidant enzymes and detoxification enzymes by regulating the Nrf2/ Keap1 signaling pathway, thereby reducing oxidative stress, apoptosis and inflammation [9,44]. Therefore, it is important to study the effect of Arg-CDs on the Keap1/Nrf2 signaling pathway in AFB1-induced liver injury. The expression of Nrf2 in the nucleus and cytoplasm suggests that AFB1-induced oxidative stress may activate the Nrf2 regulatory pathway, and Arg-CDs preconditioning may protect the liver from oxidative stress by promoting the translocation of Nrf2 from cytoplasm to nucleus. As a key transcription factor regulating the antioxidant response, Nrf2 plays a positive role in mitigating oxidative stress. However, its “dark side” has also been reported, as sustained Nrf2 activation in a kidney injury model was shown to promote apoptosis and autophagic blockage, exacerbating kidney damage [45,46]. Additionally, AFB1 metabolism depletes downstream detoxification enzymes (GSS, GCLC, GCLM) and antioxidant enzymes (SOD, CAT, HO-1, NQO-1), while Arg-CDs treatment effectively reverses these effects. Moreover, the mechanism by which AFB1 induces cellular and tissue damage involves not only oxidative stress but also inflammation and pyroptosis [47]. It is well-established that harmful endogenous or exogenous signals or stimuli, such as ROS and AFB1, can activate TXNIP and further trigger NLRP3 inflammasome self-assembly, which is closely associated with inflammation and pyroptosis. Our data indicate that Arg-CDs treatment inhibits the activation of NLRP3 inflammasomes by reducing TXNIP mRNA expression in AFB1-exposed mice, potentially blocking pyroptosis and preventing the exacerbation of liver inflammation.

Moreover, AFB1 can induce fibrosis or necrosis in the intestinal epithelium, compromising intestinal function, reducing nutrient absorption efficiency, and weakening the ability to resist pathogen invasion, ultimately leading to intestinal inflammation [48–50]. The intestinal barrier acts as a vital defense mechanism regulating the intestinal microenvironment and impeding the entry of harmful bacteria and toxins. In various pathological circumstances, gut bacteria can breach the intestinal mucosal barrier, enter the bloodstream, and reach the liver via the “gut-liver axis” pathway [51], triggering a liver cell immune response and contributing to liver disease through immune-inflammatory mechanisms [52]. Thus, we speculate that Arg-CDs pretreatment could prevent the leakage of toxic substances from intestinal by regulating intestinal barrier function. Our study shows that AFB1 exposure causes small intestine shortening, downregulation of intestinal tight junction molecules, and upregulation of pro-inflammatory factors, which was reversed after Arg-CDs intervention.

In this study, a novel carbon nanodot, Arg-CDs, synthesized from arginine and citric acid, have demonstrated remarkable free radical scavenging ability and biocompatibility (Fig. 7 ). Experiments conducted in nematodes have showed that the good biocompatibility of Arg-CDs, along with its effectiveness in reducing oxidative stress induced by juglone. Moreover, in a mouse model of AFB1-induced liver injury, Arg-CDs have shown to improve liver oxidative damage and inflammatory responses, as evidenced by changes in serum and liver transaminase levels, antioxidant enzyme markers, and liver histopathology. Mechanistically, Arg-CDs have been shown to enhance antioxidant capacity and phase II detoxification mediated by GST by regulating the Nrf2 signaling pathway. Additionally, Arg-CDs inhibit the NLRP3/Caspase-1/GSDMD-mediated pyroptosis pathway, thus mitigating AFB1-induced hepatotoxicity and oxidative stress. Together, these findings underscore the significant potential of Arg-CDs as a novel therapeutic candidate for preventing and treating toxicity induced by AFB1 exposure.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Table S1
Primer sequences used in the real-time PCR
Table S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.lfs.2025.123430.

Xuejing Cao: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jiuxiang Cheng: Visualization, Methodology, Data curation. Yongshou Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jingmin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yongzhong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

This research was supported by the Launching scientific research funds of Anhui University (S020318003/009); the Natural Science Foundation of the Anhui Higher Education Institutions of China (JM Wang, No. KJ2021A0051); the postdoctoral fellow in Anhui province (9341129); and the Construction Funds of Anhui Province Joint Training Base for Postgraduates (Z010110038).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
